svg.text: math-foundations — concept dependency graph (click a node)

a.title: 00: Sets and Functions The two foundational objects: collections and rules that map elements between them.
01: Logic and Proof Propositional and predicate logic, truth tables, the basic proof strategies (direct, contrapositive, contradiction, indu
02: Counting and Combinatorics Counting principles: permutations, combinations, the pigeonhole principle, inclusion-exclusion.
03: Relations and Orderings Equivalence relations, partial and total orders, equivalence classes.
04: Groups, Rings, Fields Abstract algebraic structures with binary operations satisfying axioms.
05: Vector Spaces Sets closed under linear combinations over a field; the universal object of linear algebra.
06: Linear Maps and Matrices Structure-preserving functions between vector spaces; the matrix representation.
07: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors Directions preserved by a linear map up to scaling; the building blocks of spectral analysis.
08: Inner Product Spaces Vector spaces equipped with a notion of angle and length via an inner product.
09: Norms and Metrics Generalised notions of distance; the foundation of analysis on vector spaces.
10: Sequences and Limits Convergence of sequences; the epsilon-N definition; Cauchy sequences and completeness.
11: Continuity The epsilon-delta definition; continuity as preservation of limits; uniform vs pointwise continuity.
12: Derivatives (Univariate) The local linear approximation; the limit of a difference quotient; differentiation rules.
13: Multivariate Calculus Partial derivatives; the chain rule in multiple variables; directional derivatives.
14: Gradient and Jacobian The gradient as the vector of partial derivatives; the Jacobian as the matrix of partial derivatives of a vector-valued
15: Integration (Riemann) The Riemann integral as a limit of step-function approximations; the fundamental theorem of calculus.
16: Series and Convergence Convergence of infinite sums; absolute vs conditional; power series and Taylor expansions.
17: Sample Spaces The set of all possible outcomes of an experiment; the starting point of probability.
18: Events and Sigma-Algebras A sigma-algebra is a collection of subsets closed under complement and countable union; the rigorous basis for which eve
19: Probability Measures A function from a sigma-algebra to [0,1] satisfying countable additivity and P(Omega) = 1.
20: Conditional Probability P(A | B) = P(A and B) / P(B); the update rule for new information.
21: Independence P(A and B) = P(A) P(B); events that do not influence each other.
22: Random Variables Measurable functions from the sample space to the real line; the bridge from sets-of-outcomes to numerical values.
23: Probability Distributions The full description of a random variable: its cumulative distribution function, its discrete probability mass function,
24: Probability Density Functions A non-negative integrable function whose integral over a set gives the probability that a continuous random variable lan
25: Expectation The weighted average of a random variable's values, weighted by their probability; the integral of the random variable a
26: Variance and Covariance Second-order moments measuring spread (variance) and linear association (covariance).
27: Conditional Expectation The expected value of a random variable given the value of another (or given a sub-sigma-algebra); the foundation of reg
28: Change of Variables (Probability) How densities transform under invertible smooth maps; the Jacobian determinant appears as the volume-correction factor.
29: Ordinary Differential Equations Equations relating a function to its derivatives; the basic objects of dynamical systems.
30: Existence and Uniqueness (Picard-Lindelöf) Sufficient conditions (Lipschitz) under which an ODE has a unique solution; the foundation of well-posed dynamical syste
31: Stochastic Processes Indexed families of random variables; the time-evolution of randomness.
32: Brownian Motion / Wiener Process The canonical continuous-time stochastic process with independent Gaussian increments; the building block of stochastic
33: Stochastic Differential Equations Differential equations driven by Brownian motion; the continuous-time analogue of random walks.
34: Itô Calculus The calculus of stochastic integrals where the integrator is non-smooth (Brownian); Itô's lemma replaces the chain rule.
35: Self-Information (Surprisal) I(x) = -log p(x); the surprise associated with observing an outcome under a probability distribution.
36: Shannon Entropy H(X) = E[-log p(X)]; the average information content of a random variable; measures uncertainty.
37: Cross-Entropy H(p, q) = -E_p[log q]; the average code-length when using distribution q to encode samples drawn from p.
38: KL Divergence D_KL(p || q) = E_p[log p - log q]; the relative entropy / information gain in moving from q to p. Non-symmetric, non-met
39: Mutual Information I(X; Y) = D_KL(p(x,y) || p(x) p(y)); the reduction in uncertainty about X from observing Y.
40: Information Geometry (Fisher Metric) Treats probability distributions as points on a Riemannian manifold; the Fisher information matrix as the natural metric
41: Optimal Transport (Wasserstein) Wasserstein distance W_p(mu, nu) = inf over couplings of E[|X - Y|^p]^(1/p); the cost of optimally moving one distributi
